metadata.title: Same Non-Response, Two Opposite Fixes: Did the Drug Fail, or Never Arrive?
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Nanomedicine card 15. Source chapter: cancer-nanomedicine/chapters/06-nano-enabled-imaging-and-contrast.md (biodistribution imaging / delivery-vs-biology failure disambiguation only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, TEAL = delivery succeeded / particle reached tumor / fix the biology, CRIMSON = delivery failed / particle in wrong organ / fix the particle, gold highlighter as single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = the good outcome (particle in tumor, delivery confirmed); CRIMSON #BF3339 = the bad outcome (particle in liver/spleen, delivery failed); never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO what-each-modality-reads detail, NO FDG proxy lesson (own video), NO activatable probes, NO release-vs-target-engagement caveat. Binary only: delivery failure vs biology failure, disambiguated by one image. THE QUESTION appears on-screen in B03 as a CARD beat with the gap formula. Illustrative numbers in B11 (THE EXAMPLE) labeled as such in FACTCHECK.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: A nanoparticle drug goes into patients. Tumors don't shrink. The team's next move: swap in a more potent — and more toxic — payload.

card.sub: Did the Drug Fail, or Never Arrive?

beats.narration_text: Before that swap happened, one collaborator asked a different question: where did the particles actually go? They labeled the same particle with a fluorescent dye and imaged its path.

props.headline: Before that swap happened, one collaborator asked a different question: where…

beats.scene_description: A schematic fluorescence image of a mouse, liver glowing brightly in false-color, tumor outlined but dim — editorial newsprint collage style, desaturated warm tones
beats.image_prompt: B02, fluorescence biodistribution scan of a laboratory mouse, bright false-color hotspot in the liver region, a tumor outlined on the flank showing minimal signal, stylized biomedical schematic, desaturated editorial newsprint reproduction, flat print, no digital glow effects
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL·ai] biodistribution scan — liver lit up, tumor dim

beats.narration_text: A nanoparticle drug trial fails. The team proposes switching to a more potent payload. Before spending the money — what should they have measured first, and why can't the response endpoint tell them?

card.copy: A nanoparticle drug trial fails. The team proposes switching to a more potent payload. Before spending the money — what should they have measured first, and why can't the response endpoint tell them?

beats.narration_text: No tumor shrinkage looks identical whether the drug was too weak to kill the cells, or the particle never delivered the drug to the tumor at all. The outcome is the same. The causes are opposite.

production_viz.label: one outcome, two opposite causes
production_viz.mechanic: a single crimson box labeled NO TUMOR SHRINKAGE sits center; two arrows point up-left toward DRUG TOO WEAK and up-right toward PARTICLE NEVER ARRIVED; both arrows are crimson
production_viz.note: establish the core ambiguity before the solution

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] one outcome, two opposite causes — convergent crimson arrows

beats.narration_text: And they demand opposite fixes. If the drug was too weak — change the drug: find a better target, raise the potency. If the particle failed delivery — fix the particle: its size, its surface coating, its circulation time.

production_viz.label: opposite causes, opposite fixes
production_viz.mechanic: two columns: left DRUG FAILURE column shows fix-the-drug (TEAL chip: new target / higher potency); right DELIVERY FAILURE column shows fix-the-particle (TEAL chip: size / surface / PEG). Each column has a crimson cause label at top and a teal fix label at bottom.
production_viz.note: the fix determines which column you're in — emphasize the branching

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two columns: drug failure fix vs delivery failure fix

beats.narration_text: Swapping in a more potent drug when delivery has failed doesn't help the tumor. It sends a more toxic compound to wherever the particles actually accumulated — the liver, the spleen, the organs clearing them.

document.quote: A more potent drug in the same particle would have loaded those organs with more toxin.

beats.scene_description: large serif quote card; gold highlighter sweeps 'more toxin' as it is spoken
beats.new_visual_element: [DOCUMENT] quote: more potent drug -> more toxin in wrong organs

beats.narration_text: The experiment that breaks the ambiguity is biodistribution imaging. Label the identical particle with a contrast agent — a fluorescent dye, an iron oxide core for MRI, a radiolabel for PET — and measure where it goes.

production_viz.label: label the particle, track it
production_viz.mechanic: a single particle schematic at center; three small annotation chips fan out from it: FLUORESCENT DYE, IRON OXIDE (MRI), RADIOLABEL (PET) — each in SLATE chips with serif labels. Then a scanning line sweeps across a body outline.
production_viz.note: manim move: scan — the body outline reveals as the scan line moves. No modality detail; just the principle that you label and track.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] particle with imaging labels + body scan sweep

beats.narration_text: The result is a biodistribution map. Particles concentrated in the liver and spleen: the immune system cleared them before they reached the tumor. Particles in the tumor at meaningful levels: delivery succeeded. The ambiguity is gone.

props.headline: The result is a biodistribution map. Particles concentrated in the liver…

beats.scene_description: two side-by-side body silhouette panels on newsprint background — left panel liver/spleen bright (delivery failed), right panel tumor bright (delivery confirmed)
beats.image_prompt: B08, editorial biomedical schematic showing two side-by-side body silhouette panels on aged newsprint background. Left panel: mouse torso outline with bright false-color accumulation in liver and spleen, labeled LIVER/SPLEEN, crimson-toned. Right panel: identical outline with bright signal in tumor mass on flank, labeled TUMOR, teal-green-toned. Flat editorial print style, desaturated, documentary diagram aesthetic
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL·ai] body silhouette: liver/spleen crimson vs tumor teal — two outcomes

beats.narration_text: If the particles are in the liver and spleen — the reticuloendothelial system cleared them. That's an engineering problem in the particle. PEGylation insufficient? Particle too large? Surface charge attracting immune recognition? Fix the particle.

production_viz.label: delivery failure -> fix the particle
production_viz.mechanic: CRIMSON column: top chip PARTICLES IN LIVER/SPLEEN; below, three teal chips stack with arrows: FIX PEGylation, FIX SIZE, FIX SURFACE CHARGE. Each chip fades in with a small left-pointing arrow indicating the engineering lever.
production_viz.note: no modality detail — just the engineering logic tree

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] delivery failure column: three engineering fix levers

beats.narration_text: If the particles reached the tumor — the delivery chain worked. Now the question is payload and biology: did the drug hit its target? That's a pharmacology problem. Change the drug, not the particle.

document.quote: Particles in the tumor means delivery succeeded. The payload or biology is the limiting step.

beats.scene_description: large serif quote card; gold highlighter sweeps 'delivery succeeded' as it is spoken; sets up the biology-fix logic
beats.new_visual_element: [DOCUMENT] quote: delivery succeeded -> the payload is the question now

beats.narration_text: Here's an illustrative example. Two programs run identical nanoparticles in ovarian cancer. Program A measures tumor response only: seven percent, trial closed. Program B runs a ten-patient biodistribution cohort first. Imaging shows more than seventy-five percent of tracer in the liver at four hours, less than three percent in the tumor. Program B redesigns its PEG coating. Its next efficacy trial: twenty-one percent response. Same particle. Program B knew why it failed.

production_viz.label: two programs, same particle — illustrative
production_viz.mechanic: two side-by-side columns. Left PROGRAM A: top chip 7% RESPONSE, large crimson chip CLOSED. Right PROGRAM B: top chip BIODISTRIBUTION FIRST, then a bar chart (crimson bar LIVER 75%+ vs teal bar TUMOR <3%), then chip REDESIGN PEG, then chip 21% RESPONSE. Illustrative label at top of scene.
production_viz.note: ALL numbers (7%, 75%, 3%, 21%, 10 patients) are illustrative — labeled as such. This is THE EXAMPLE act.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two program columns with bar chart and outcome chips — illustrative

beats.narration_text: Same non-response. Two opposite fixes. Biodistribution imaging is not a separate step from drug development — it is the instrument that converts an ambiguous failure into a diagnosis. Particles in the wrong organ: fix the particle. Particles in the tumor: fix the drug.

card.copy: Measure where the particle went before changing the drug.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard — measure delivery before changing the drug

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Cancer Nanomedicine series.
